questions.prompt: Arrange, Act, Assert structures each test as:
choices: three assertions minimum, one per section
setup shared by the whole suite, then a sequence of related actions
build one world, perform ONE behavior, check its postcondition
act first, then arrange the expected output for comparison

questions.prompt: A test reads the real system clock and fails intermittently. The principled fix:
choices: auto-rerun flaky tests and accept the majority verdict
make the clock a parameter; production passes the real one, tests pass a fake
add margin — sleep long enough that timing always settles
widen the assertions until any timing passes

questions.prompt: The difference between a fake and a mock:
choices: a fake is a cheap working implementation; a mock verifies that expected calls happened
they are two names for the same thing
a mock is suitable for production use; a fake is not
a fake records calls for later assertions; a mock returns canned data
it raises AttributeError on any method you did not configure
it is too slow for large test suites
it cannot stand in for functions, only for classes
it answers everything — typos included — with a truthy Mock, so a careless test cannot fail

questions.prompt: A test asserting bag._counts == {"a": 2} is a smell because:
choices: it welds the test to the representation; honest refactors now break tests
underscored attributes cannot be read from other modules
dict equality is unreliable across Python versions
tests should never assert equality, only types

questions.prompt: The load-bearing difference between a list and an iterator:
choices: iterators support len(); lists do not
a list can be walked any number of times; an iterator IS its own progress — one shot
lists are lazy; iterators are eager
a for loop accepts a list, but you must call iter() yourself before looping over an iterator
runs the body eagerly and caches the yielded values
runs the body up to the first yield, then pauses
runs none of the body — execution starts at the first next()
starts the body concurrently in a background thread

questions.prompt: In a well-shaped program, dependency arrows point:
choices: from volatile details (I/O, formatting) toward the stable domain model
outward from the model to the modules that use it
both ways between any two cooperating modules
toward the modules that change most often
